name: Bob Dylan
description: The Nobel laureate off the record — Dylan on art, fame, and freedom in his own words.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: If someone had ever told me that I had the slightest chance of winning the Nobel Prize, I would have to think that I'd have about the same odds as standing on the moon.
quotes.notes: Opening of Dylan's acceptance remarks, read on his behalf at the Nobel banquet.

quotes.content: Not once have I ever had the time to ask myself, 'Are my songs literature?'
quotes.notes: The closing line of his banquet speech, thanking the Swedish Academy for answering the question for him.

quotes.content: As a performer I've played for 50,000 people and I've played for 50 people and I can tell you that it is harder to play for 50 people.
quotes.notes: Dylan on the intimacy and difficulty of small audiences.

quotes.content: I began to think about William Shakespeare, the great literary figure. I would reckon he thought of himself as a dramatist. The thought that he was writing literature couldn't have entered his head.
quotes.notes: Dylan compares his own songwriting to Shakespeare's practical, working-craftsman mindset.

quotes.content: His words were written for the stage. Meant to be spoken not read.
quotes.notes: On Shakespeare, drawing the parallel that songs, like plays, are meant to be performed.

quotes.content: Our songs are alive in the land of the living.
quotes.notes: Near the close of his recorded Nobel lecture, on songs being meant to be sung, not read.

quotes.content: These songs didn't come out of thin air. I didn't just make them up out of whole cloth.
quotes.source: MusiCares Person of the Year speech (2015)
quotes.notes: Dylan crediting the folk and traditional-music roots of his catalog.

quotes.content: It all came out of traditional music: traditional folk music, traditional rock 'n' roll and traditional big-band swing orchestra music.
quotes.source: MusiCares Person of the Year speech (2015)
quotes.notes: Continuing his account of how his songwriting was seeded by tradition, not invented from nothing.

quotes.content: I learned lyrics and how to write them from listening to folk songs.
quotes.source: MusiCares Person of the Year speech (2015)
quotes.notes: Dylan on the folk repertoire as his songwriting apprenticeship.

quotes.content: They gave me the code for everything that's fair game, that everything belongs to everyone.
quotes.source: MusiCares Person of the Year speech (2015)
quotes.notes: On what the old folk standards taught him about the commons of song.

quotes.content: Oh, I think of myself more as a song and dance man, y'know.
quotes.notes: His famous deflection when asked whether he was primarily a singer or a poet.

quotes.content: The words are just as important as the music. There would be no music without the words.
quotes.notes: Dylan on the primacy of lyrics, adding that he writes the words first.

quotes.content: I like to think of it more in terms of vision music—it's mathematical music.
quotes.notes: His answer when asked what to call his music, resisting the 'folk-rock' label.

quotes.content: I welcome them—with open arms.
quotes.notes: Reply when asked what he thinks of people who analyze his songs.

quotes.content: As a constitutional re-play of mass production.
quotes.notes: His cryptic, deadpan definition of folk music at the press conference.

quotes.content: I made a bargain with it, you know, long time ago. And I'm holding up my end.
quotes.notes: Explaining why he still tours; he later names the other party as 'the chief commander.'

quotes.content: All those early songs were almost magically written.
quotes.notes: Dylan reflecting on his 1960s output, which he says he couldn't write today.

quotes.content: I don't know how I got to write those songs.
quotes.notes: On the mystery of his own early creative period.

quotes.content: A song is like a dream, and you have to make it come true.
quotes.notes: From Dylan's memoir, describing the elusive work of writing a song.

quotes.content: I had very little in common with and knew even less about a generation that I was supposed to be the voice of.
quotes.notes: Dylan rejecting the 'voice of a generation' mantle placed on him in the 1960s.

quotes.content: I was sick of the way my lyrics had been extrapolated, their meanings subverted into polemics.
quotes.notes: On his weariness with being read as a political spokesman.

quotes.content: Folk music was a reality of a more brilliant dimension.
quotes.notes: Dylan on the imaginative world he found in traditional song.

quotes.content: It was a strange world ahead that would unfold, a thunderhead of a world with jagged lightning edges.
quotes.notes: A closing image from the memoir on the future he sensed at the start of his career.

quotes.content: Most of the other performers tried to put themselves across, rather than the song.
quotes.notes: Dylan on what set the singers he admired apart in the early folk scene.

quotes.content: I want to needle them.
quotes.notes: His reply when asked why his songs are so hard on people.

quotes.content: Destiny is a feeling you have that you know something about yourself nobody else does.
quotes.notes: Widely attributed to Chronicles, but exact wording not confirmed against the primary text (wording axis).
